FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study; n=4,641 ages 9-12 at baseline, Year 1, Year 2 follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) = self-reported hours on non-school screen time (includes TV, videos, gaming, texting, video chatting, social networking). Qualifies as smartphone/app use + social media per B1

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs) with regression coefficients; bidirectional associations tested. E.g., β = -0.10 [95%CI: -0.16, -0.03], p = 0.004 for SMA → sleep duration

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients with 95% CIs reported throughout: SMA-sleep (β = -0.10, -0.11), externalizing-sleep (β = 0.11), externalizing-SMA (β = 0.07), mediation indirect effect (0.032 [95%CI: 0.003, 0.067])

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; longitudinal CLPMs with discovery (n=2,359) and replication (n=2,282) samples

FT_IC6.evidence: Published full-length report; not a preprint/abstract/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: SMA is independent variable (IV) predicting sleep and psychopathology (non-SMA outcomes); not SMA-as-DV. Violates condition (a). Cross-lagged models are bidirectional per B20, which disqualifies EC7
notes: Bidirectional CLPMs show sleep → SMA and SMA → sleep; externalizing symptoms mediate thalamus-PFC-brainstem covariation to SMA. Study examines complex temporal dynamics across three time points

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study; N=10,828 youth (47.8% female), mean age 9.9 years at baseline, 10.9 years at follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time: self-reported hours/day on six screen types (streaming TV/movies, streaming videos, videogames, texting, video chatting, social networking sites) on weekdays and weekend days separately. Dichotomized as >2 vs ≤2 hours/day. Qualifies as smartphone use (texting, video chat) + social media per B1

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel logistic regression with study site as random effect; odds ratios (ORs) with 95% CIs reported. E.g., OR 1.6 (95% CI 1.1–2.2) for weekend screen time → withdrawn/depressed symptoms in females

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric ORs with 95% CIs throughout tables: female sleep → withdrawn/depressed OR 0.6 (95% CI 0.4–0.8); female weekend screen time → withdrawn/depressed OR 1.6 (95% CI 1.1–2.2)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical longitudinal analysis; one-year prospective design from baseline to follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Published full-length original article; not preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is IV predicting internalizing symptoms (non-SM outcomes). Condition (a) fails: paper has SM → non-SM-outcome analysis, not SM-as-DV only
notes: Longitudinal associations unidirectional (behavior → internalizing symptoms); gender-stratified analyses show associations in females only for weekend screen time and withdrawn/depressed symptoms

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study; N=10,544 U.S. youth, baseline mean age 9.9 (0.03) years; 48.9% female, 45.4% non-White; one-year follow-up (2017–2018 to 2018–2019)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time: self-reported daily minutes of streaming, gaming, and socializing on weekdays and weekend days separately. Three distinct screen types with separate coefficients reported. Socializing qualifies as social media use per B1; gaming + social media as separate coefficients per B6

FT_IC3.evidence: Isotemporal substitution regression models and Compositional Data Analysis (CoDA). Regression β coefficients with 95% CIs reported. E.g., in females: replacing 30 min socializing with 30 min physical activity β = -0.03 [95% CI -0.05, -0.002]

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients with 95% CIs throughout: females replacing socializing with PA β = -0.03 [-0.05, -0.002], replacing streaming β = -0.03 [-0.05, -0.01], replacing gaming β = -0.03 [-0.06, -0.01]; males CoDA β = 0.05 [0.02, 0.08]

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical longitudinal analysis; baseline (2017–2018) and one-year follow-up (2018–2019) with sex-stratified isotemporal substitution and CoDA models

FT_IC6.evidence: Published full-length original research article; not preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is IV predicting BMI z-score (non-SM outcome). Condition (a) fails: paper contains screen-time → BMI analyses, not SM-as-DV only
notes: Sex-stratified analyses using two complementary statistical methods (isotemporal substitution and CoDA); results differ somewhat by method; different screen types show different associations with BMI
